phrasalVerbs.definition: To examine someone or something in order to be certain that everything is correct, true, or satisfactory.
examples: Can you check out this report before I send it?
I'll check out the new restaurant everyone's talking about.
The doctor will check out your symptoms.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To meet someone or find something by chance.
examples: I came across an old photo while cleaning.
She came across as very friendly during the interview.
Have you come across any useful websites?

phrasalVerbs.definition: To become available to buy or see; to have a particular result or end in a particular way.
examples: Her new book comes out next month.
The photos came out really well.
When does the new phone model come out?

phrasalVerbs.definition: To be connected to a place by telephone.
examples: I've been trying to get through to the office all morning.
The lines were busy, but I finally got through.
Can you get through to customer service?

phrasalVerbs.definition: To give someone something, for example a message, that someone else has given you.
examples: Could you pass on a message to your manager?
She passed on the information to the team.
Please pass on my thanks to everyone involved.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To write down information or a statement.
examples: Let me take down your phone number.
The secretary took down the meeting notes.
I need to take down the details before I forget.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To discuss a problem or a plan.
examples: We should talk over the proposal before making a decision.
Let's talk it over during lunch.
They talked over their differences and made up.

fillInExercises.sentence: Can you ___ ___ this document for any errors?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Check out' means to examine something to make sure it's correct.

fillInExercises.sentence: I ___ across your old letters while cleaning the attic.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Come across' means to find something by chance.

fillInExercises.sentence: I tried calling but couldn't ___ through to anyone.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get through' means to successfully connect by phone.

fillInExercises.sentence: Please ___ ___ my congratulations to your sister.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pass on' means to give a message from one person to another.

fillInExercises.sentence: Let me ___ down your address before you go.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Take down' means to write down information.

fillInExercises.sentence: We need to ___ ___ the budget before the meeting.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Talk over' means to discuss a problem or plan.

fillInExercises.sentence: Her new album ___ out next week.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Come out' means to become available to buy.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Did you manage to [get through] to the supplier?

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: Yes, finally! I [took down] all the delivery details.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Great. Can you [pass on] the information to the warehouse?

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: Sure. I also [came across] some old invoices we need to [check out].

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Let's [talk over] the invoice issue after lunch.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: Perfect. The new ordering system [comes out] next month, so this might get easier.

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: I [came across] some interesting news today.

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Really? [Pass on] the details! What is it?

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: A new study [came out] about learning languages.

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: I'd love to [check out] the full report.

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: I'll [take down] the link and send it to you.

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Thanks! We should [talk over] what we find later.

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: Let me know when you [get through] reading it!
